turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: Hi, thanks for seeing me. To be honest, I've been having some really bad abdominal pain, mostly on my left side, and it's been getting worse over the past few weeks. It feels like cramps, but they're not just random cramps - they're constant.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: Y-yes, it has. And it's not just the pain, doc. I've also been having bloody stools with mucus in them, which is terrifying me. The blood is bright red and mixed in with my stool, and it's happening more often than ever.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: Yes, doc... after I have a bowel movement, especially when there's blood in it, I feel this overwhelming sense of urgency and discomfort, like my stomach is still trying to get rid of something. And sometimes, even after I've gone, I feel like I didn't completely empty my bowels, which makes me anxious all over again.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: To be honest, doc, I've been feeling really fatigued lately, especially at work. It's like my body just can't keep up with my usual energy level, and it's affecting my performance.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: The pain is usually centered around my lower abdomen, but sometimes it radiates to my knees and ankles, where I feel a dull ache, especially when I'm moving around. It's not like they're swollen or anything, just this nagging discomfort that's hard to ignore.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: To be honest, doc, I've been experiencing a bit of fatigue lately, especially at work, and it's affecting my performance. It feels like my body is just not able to keep up with its usual energy level, which is really concerning me.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: No, doc, there's no significant swelling in my knees or ankles that I've noticed. It's more of a dull ache, like I said earlier, and it doesn't seem to be getting worse, but it's still really bothering me.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: Actually, doc, I haven't experienced any nausea or vomiting, thankfully. But what I have noticed is that my bowel movements have become really frequent - I'm talking 8-10 times a day, which is way more than usual for me. It's like my body can't hold anything down anymore, and it's been super uncomfortable.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: Doc, to be honest, I've been feeling really fatigued lately, especially at work. It's like my body just can't keep up with its usual energy level, and it's affecting my performance. As for my appetite, I've actually lost about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past three weeks due to my irregular meals and frequent bowel movements, which has been really concerning me.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: Doc, yes... I've actually noticed that I get a low-grade fever about 2-3 times a week. It's not extremely high, just around 37.8-38.0°C, but it's definitely something that's been worrying me. And to be honest, it's also been waking me up at night because of the diarrhea, so I'm getting pretty concerned about what might be going on with my body.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient:
Doc, actually, the only significant medical condition I had in the past was an appendectomy when I was 16 years old, and that went smoothly without any complications. Other than that, I'm relatively healthy, although my older sister did get diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30, which is a bit concerning for me given our family history.

However, I do want to mention that I haven't seen a doctor in over two years, and I haven't had any recent bloodwork or tests done. That might be relevant to understanding what's going on with my symptoms.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: Doc, I've actually been trying to manage my symptoms on my own with loperamide (Imodium) for the past two weeks, but it's only provided minimal relief. I was hoping that seeing a doctor would help me figure out what's going on and find a better solution.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: To be honest, doc, my eating habits have been pretty irregular lately due to my long work hours and stress from symptoms and work. I've mostly been relying on takeout and convenience foods because it's hard for me to keep up with cooking healthy meals, especially when I'm not feeling well.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: Doc, exercise has actually been tough for me lately due to my fatigue and frequent bowel movements, but I try to stay active by taking short walks around my apartment complex when I can. It's not much, but it helps me feel a bit better, you know?

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been on my mind lately because of our family history and my own symptoms. I'm worried that I might have something similar, which is why I've been coming to see you today.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yes, doc... ever since my sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease, I've had this constant fear in the back of my mind that I might be next. It's like a ticking time bomb waiting to go off, and every symptom I experience makes me more anxious about what could be happening inside my body.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: Doc, yes... I'm a bit of an occasional drinker, I'd say. I usually have 2-3 beers most weeknights when I unwind after work, but over the past month, I've actually increased my intake to 4-5 beers per night due to stress from symptoms and work. It's not something I'm proud of, but it's been a way for me to cope with everything that's been going on.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, doc, I don't smoke and I've never used any recreational drugs. I'm actually pretty careful about taking care of myself in that regard.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: Doc, to be honest with you, I've been so worried about what might be going on inside my body that I haven't wanted to admit some things. But now that we're talking, I feel like it's getting too much for me to keep all this bottled up. There are times when the pain is so bad and the diarrhea is so frequent that I wonder if I'm losing control of my own body. It sounds silly, but sometimes I feel like I'm just existing instead of living, you know?

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: Doc, yes... I think what's really been holding me back from talking openly about all this is the fear of hearing something bad. I've been trying to avoid thinking about it because I don't know how I'd cope with a serious diagnosis like Crohn's disease or another underlying condition that could be causing my symptoms. But at the same time, not knowing what's going on and just living in this state of uncertainty is taking a toll on me emotionally and mentally. I guess what I'm trying to say is that I'm scared, but I also want to know the truth so we can work together to find a solution.
